# A sealed object can end up out of sync with the audit log when it is restored
# without the log (a `git checkout -- <object>`, a merge, a manual swap). The
# on-disk root hash then no longer matches the log and a full verify fails. The
# post-checkout hook loads with no-disk verify, reveals from the on-disk object,
# and re-seals to record it - reconciling the repo instead of aborting.
#
# We create the mismatch with a plain file copy rather than `git checkout` so the
# real post-checkout hook doesn't fire and heal it before we can invoke it.

exec git init
exec git config user.email test@sesam.dev
exec git config user.name 'Sesam Test'

exec sesam init --user admin
cp secret.env app.env
exec sesam add app.env --group admin
exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'v1 of the secret'
cp .sesam/objects/app.env.sesam v1.sealed

# Rotate + re-seal the secret: new object and a new seal root hash.
cp secret2.env app.env
exec sesam seal

# Restore the v1 object underneath the (v2) log -> stale on-disk root hash.
cp v1.sealed .sesam/objects/app.env.sesam

# A normal full verify fails on the on-disk root-hash mismatch…
! exec sesam verify
stderr 'root hash mismatch'

# …but the post-checkout hook loads no-disk, reveals the restored (v1) object,
# and re-seals to reconcile the log.
rm app.env
exec sesam hook post-checkout HEAD HEAD 0
exists app.env
grep 'super-secret' app.env

# The log now matches the on-disk object, so a full verify passes again.
exec sesam verify

-- secret.env --
TOKEN=super-secret
-- secret2.env --
TOKEN=rotated-secret
